observations.recommendation:
Facilitate the creation on an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of  properties of the municipality. The actual count could be undertaken any month during the current  year, but it has to be updated at year-end. A copy of the Inventory Report has to be submitted to COA not later than January 31of each year.
Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports. Any discrepancies shall be verified and adjusted accordingly.  Recipient of any missing PPEs be held accountable.
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Cards.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control.
During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive facilitate the processing of the TCT for all their untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to show conclusive proof of ownership of the LGU over the parcels of land.
We have further recommended that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order No. 37-09 dated October 19, 2009 of the Department of Finance “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

We have recommended that the Accounting Unit exhaust all possible means to determine the details and dates of acquisition of the LGU properties recorded under Other Infrastructure Assets account. After which, compute and record immediately the depreciation in the books of accounts to present fairly the carrying value of the PPE account in the financial statements.
We have also recommended that adjusting entries be made to restate the depreciable cost of the Office Equipment account by re-computing the residual value which should be equivalent to at least five percent (5%) of the cost of the asset in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We have recommended that the accounting and supply records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements.
Ensure that a physical inventory is regularly conducted on inventory items for each semester and the result of the physical count is reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting, management should strictly adhere and be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant has to reconcile with the Municipal Treasurer relative to the amount of delinquent local taxes and disclosed the correct amount in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting or correcting entries and record the 15% share of the Office of the Building Official (OBO) from collections of Building Permits as Trust Liabilities. Strict adherence of the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units is further recommended for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Accounting Office to determine the discrepancies between the Due from Other Funds account and Due to Other Funds account and to make necessary adjusting or correcting entries to reconcile the two accounts.

We have recommended that Management stop from charging previous year’s expenditures to current appropriation pursuant to Section 119 of PD 1445. We have also recommended that before the year end, the Municipal Accountant should set a deadline for the submission of all claims of officials and employees, and creditors in order to record the obligations of the agency and recognized the expenditures on the year they were actually incurred to avoid obligating the same in succeeding year’s appropriation. Non-compliance thereof shall result to the issuance of notice of disallowance.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting or correcting entries and strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
We have also recommended that request for monetization of fifty percent (50%) or more of accumulated leave credits of an official or employee be approved only by the Local Chief Executive for reasons allowed by law. Strict compliance with the provisions of Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules on Leave is further recommended.

We have recommended that the Municipal Administration stop the practice of granting cash advances to officials and employees who are not designated disbursing officers and for purposes other than those allowed under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
We have also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to make payment thru check directly in the name of the supplier or creditor instead of granting cash advances.  Strict compliance with the provisions of the above-cited COA Circular and RA 9184 is further recommended to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance.

We have recommended that the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan immediately stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions under Section 447(a) of R.A. No. 7160 to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance in audit. We have also recommended that the appropriated funds for donations under the said office be transferred to the MSWDO whose task is to implement such programs, projects and activities.

We have recommended that management strictly comply with the maximum allowable monthly contribution which is One Hundred Pesos (₱100.00) as employer’s share per covered employee to Home Development Mutual Fund (HDMF)/PAG-IBIG Fund pursuant to Section 7 of Republic Act 9679 to avoid excessive payments which prejudice the financial interest of the local government unit.

We have recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 7.5 of Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 in the granting of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) to members of the local sanggunian to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
We have further recommended that the prescribed minimum number of regular session which shall be once a week for the Sangguniang Bayan be strictly observed in compliance with Section 52 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160.

We have recommended that Management stop the practice of granting Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials who are assigned with and presently using government vehicles pursuant to Section 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

We have recommended that management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% Development Fund is utilized within the purposes expressly prescribed by law in order to achieve desirable socio-economic development  and environmental outcomes and to avoid possible sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance therewith.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting or correcting entries and strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
We have further recommended that the Municipal Administration stop the practice of granting cash advances to individuals who are neither appointed/designated as disbursing officers nor employees of the LGU in compliance with Section 4.1.5 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its Special Education Fund in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We have recommended that Management require the current Municipal School Board (MSB) to reorganize the composition of the School Board and to include the SK Federation President for proper representation therein.  Further, cause the removal of the Municipal Budget Officer who is not a qualified member pursuant to the provisions of Section 98(3) of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 7160.

We have recommended that Management conduct physical inventory of PPEs to determine the actual insurable properties of the LGU.  Submit updated inventory report of properties with the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the updated amount of premium for the insurance of all insurable assets. Then, allocate additional budget for the payment of insurance premium when necessary.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approve by him, and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have recommended that management release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 of Republic Act 7160 also known and cited as the Local Government Code of 1991 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which are beneficial to its constituents.

We have recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
We have also recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

We have recommended that Management exert concerted effort to collect delinquent realty taxes by applying Section 254 of RA 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management may consider to impose civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of R.A. 7160 to enforce and intensify the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums.

We have recommend that Management stop from charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues and promoting women’s empowerment and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01. The GAD Focal Person should assist in determining GAD related issues in the locality which will be considered in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.
We have also recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point and Municipal Budget Officer to submit explanation/s on why the amount of above-mentioned expenditures reflected in the GAD Accomplishment Report exceeded the amount of appropriations provided therefor to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to –
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues that need immediate attention and action. We have also recommended that members of the LCPC prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that prior year’s unexpended balance of LDRRMF is included in the revised Annual Investment Program (AIP) duly approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱4,744,188.83 under the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and revert back the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities – DRRMF for CY 2012 and CY 2013 from Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which could be used for other social services of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of General Services Officer, to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended  that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to implement the following audit recommendations, to wit:

Maintain individual subsidiary ledger for all accounts and exert diligent effort to locate the necessary supporting documents  to determine the exact age of the receivable accounts to establish the validity and accuracy of the respective receivable account balances presented in the financial statements.

When these accounts are confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for ten (10) years or more  and where settlements or collectability could no longer be ascertained, be guided by the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers under COA Circular 216-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Require the liquidation of cash advances granted to principals of various public schools in Manaoag by issuing a demand letter. Ensure that no cash advances shall be granted to these principals unless the previous cash advances have been duly liquidated.

Reclassify the cash advances granted to the principals from Due from Officers and Employees account to Due from NGAs account because these principals were not officers or employees of the Municipality but employees of DepEd which is a national government agency.

Transfer to the Special Trust Fund account the unexpended/unobligated balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF)  for CY 2016 with a total amount of P3,972,692.51.  For  CY 2017, ensure that any unutilized fund related to LDRRMF be transferred to the Special Trust Fund account at the end of the year pursuant to Sections 4.4 and 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction:
Actual 5% of LDRRM Funds is recomputed at the end of the year because we compute this based on the actual income of the year.
 Rest assured, the fund is transferred the following year upon computing the exact amount.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended  that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies and materials by using the perpetual inventory method except for purchases  out of the petty cash fund which is to be recorded as an outright expense. Issuance of supplies and materials shall be supported by a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) which will be consolidated in a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). It shall be the basis of the Accounting Unit to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the monthly issuance of supplies and materials in the books of accounts. 

We have recommended further that the Local Chief Executive shall require periodic physical inventory of supplies or property.  Physical count of inventory items by type shall be conducted semestrally and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). This shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

For other concerns on supply management and accounting thereof, please be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the municipality, we have recommended  that at the end of every month, the agency  submit to the COA Audit Team Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Official Fuel Consumption for audit purposes as required by COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977. In addition,  the following  measures  are hereby recommended:

Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Tickets are duly approved.

Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are dated and properly numbered, and these are recorded in a logbook for control purposes. A separate file for the Driver’s Trip Tickets are to be maintained.
Ensure that purpose and destination are properly indicated.
Ensure that number of kilometers travelled and fuel consumption are also indicated.
Ensure Driver’s Trip Tickets are properly signed by passengers
Ensure that dates of travel per  Driver’s Trip Ticket are consistent with the dates indicated in the  Travel Order and Certificate of Appearance.
The Head of the Agency, Municipal Accountant and Head of the Requesting Unit have to exercise  judgment and discretion in determining the reasonableness of fuel consumption.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended  that the Municipal Accountant revisit the features and function of the electronic accounting system and be acquainted with the functions it can perform. We also have recommended  that the Accounting Office refrain from using the  old Chart of Accounts in recording transactions in the JEV. Initial recognition of transactions in the JEV should be in accordance with prescribed account titles and account codes under the COA Circular No. 2015-009 to ensure consistency of account titles and account codes used from JEVs, journals, ledgers, trial balance up to  presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended  that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the DSWD are adhered to, which include compliance on proper and complete documentation, before effecting the payment.

We also have recommended  that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes  to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. 

We have also recommended  that a separate Petty Cash Fund with a reasonable amount be set up to facilitate the payment of financial assistance for burial, transportation, medical assistance and other emergency needs with an amount not exceeding P3,000.00. Pursuant to the general policies of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008, these shall be paid in outright cash directly to the client. It will also eliminate numerous reimbursements of financial assistance.

However noble the intention of the local government unit in extending financial assistance to the constituents, barangays, schools and various entities in the municipality, compliance with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations governing the payment of financial assistance should be observed.
observations.managementAction:
Rectify practice of financial assistance to individuals, but instead assistance is given to barangays, schools and various entities in the municipality where the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations governing the payment of financial assistance are observed.

No more issuance of financial assistance to individuals but to the barangays, schools, organizations and various entities, except churches / religious organizations.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended  that mix waste shall be transported first in the MRF  for final sorting, segregation, composting, and recycling.  And it is only the resulting residual wastes shall be transferred to a long-term storage or disposal facility or sanitary landfill. We also have recommended  that management involve the participation of the barangay officials in information dissemination on the importance of segregations of waste at source, and to forewarn them that incineration of solid waste  be avoided because it is prohibited by law.
observations.managementAction:
The 10 year Solid Waste Management Plan (2017-2026) has been conditionally approved by the National Solid Waste Management Board, subject to submission of additional Waste Analysis Classification System. Full implementation is expected to be completed by end of 2018.

Extensive Information, Education Campaign will be implemented at the Household levels through the Barangay Solid Wastes Management Councils. 

MRFs located in cluster barangays will be activated to minimize solid wastes in the current dumpsite/ new sanitary landfill.  Only residuals will be allowed to be processed at the new Sanitary Landfill. 

The old Open Dumpsite will be closed through the technical assistance from the DENR-EMB. | Additional documentary requirements on the 10year SWM Plan is underway.
Purchase of lot for the Sanitary Landfill completed
Design of the Sanitary Landfill will be coordinated with DENR-EMB.
Topography of the new Sanitary Landfill in being drafted.
